statement.markdown:
Given an integer N (2 ≤ N ≤ 10^12), you have to output its prime factorisation in the format "p1^e1 * p2^e2 * ... * pk^ek" where p1 < p2 < ... < pk are prime numbers and ei are their respective positive exponents. The factorisation must be printed without any spaces except the single space surrounding each asterisk (*) that separates the terms. If a prime appears with exponent 1, the exponent should be omitted (e.g., "7" instead of "7^1"). You will be given multiple test cases. For each test case, output the factorisation on a separate line.

The solution must run efficiently for the given constraints. Standard integer arithmetic and fast prime testing/sieving techniques are required to handle the upper bound of N within the time limits.
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains a single integer T, the number of test cases.
The next T lines each contain a single integer N.
statement.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the prime factorisation of N as described in the statement.
statement.notes: The asterisk (*) is used as the multiplication symbol and must be surrounded by a single space on both sides. No trailing spaces are allowed at the end of a line. The primes must be listed in strictly increasing order.

examples.explanation: 60 = 2^2 * 3 * 5, 13 is a prime so it is printed alone, and 1000003 is also a prime.

hints.text: Start by converting the input number to a string, then use a for loop to iterate from 2 to the square root of N.
For each iteration, check if the string length is greater than 1 and if the last character is a digit. If yes, check if the number is divisible by the last digit and update the exponent accordingly.
If the number is divisible, continue the loop and update the prime number and exponent accordingly. If not, move to the next iteration.
After the loop, sort the prime numbers in descending order and print the factorization as per the given format.
To optimize, use a set to store the primes encountered and check divisibility only once for each prime.

editorial.title: Optimizing Prime Factorization
editorial.intuition: The key observation is that any composite factor of N must have a prime factor less than or equal to sqrt(N). This allows us to only iterate up to sqrt(N) to find all prime factors.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve checking divisibility of N by all numbers up to N, resulting in a time complexity of O(N), which is impractical for large inputs.
editorial.betterSolution: By iterating up to sqrt(N) and dividing N by the found prime factors, we can efficiently find all prime factors.
editorial.optimalSolution: The provided solution with a time complexity of O(sqrt(N) + T * log(N)) and space complexity of O(log(N)) is optimal.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution follows from the fact that any prime factor p of N must satisfy p <= sqrt(N) if N/p > p, and N/p = p if N is a perfect square. The solution iterates over all possible prime factors up to sqrt(N) and accumulates their exponents.
editorial.dryRun: Testing the solution with sample inputs, such as N = 100 = 2^2 * 5, verifies its correctness.
commonMistakes: Not stopping the iteration at sqrt(N), leading to O(N) time complexity
Not handling the case where N is a prime number greater than sqrt(N)
Not accumulating the exponents of prime factors correctly
Not sorting the prime factors before outputting
Not handling the case where N is a perfect square
